question.text: What distinguishes an ETF (Exchange Traded Fund) from an Index Fund?

answer.text: ETFs are actively managed while Index Funds are passively managed
ETFs track a benchmark index while Index Funds do not
ETFs are listed on a stock exchange and have real-time NAV, while Index Funds are not listed and have daily NAV
ETFs have a higher expense ratio than Index Funds

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Both ETFs and Index Funds are passive instruments that track a benchmark index. The key difference is that ETFs are listed on stock exchanges and trade in real-time (with real-time NAV), while Index Funds are bought/sold at the daily declared NAV like regular mutual funds. **The Trap:** Options A and B are wrong because both ETFs and Index Funds are passively managed and both track an index. Option D is generally the opposite — ETFs typically have lower expense ratios than Index Funds. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A (MF Distributors) extensively covers ETFs and Index Funds as part of the mutual fund product suite. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** ETFs require a demat account and a broker to trade, while Index Funds can be bought directly from the AMC. This practical difference matters when advising retail investors.
